context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 4


Problem 4.1 (25 pts)

In the beaded string shown in Figure 1, the interval between neighboring beads is a, and the distance from the end beads to the wall is a/2. All the beads have mass m and are constrained to move only vertically in the plane of the paper. The strings are massless with constant string tension T.

[Figure 1: Beads on a string — beads numbered 1, 2, 3, ..., N−1, N spaced a apart, with the end beads a distance a/2 from each fixed wall.]

(a) Show that the physics of the left-hand wall can be incorporated by going to an infinite system and requiring the boundary condition A1 = −A0.
(b) Find the analogous boundary condition for the right-hand wall.
(c) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies for the finite system.


Problem 4.2 (25 pts)

A physicist was trying to understand a system with two coupled torsional pendula. First, she tried to write down the equations of motion of the pendula in terms of θ1 and θ2, which are the angles with respect to the equilibrium positions of the pendula. She found that the interaction matrix M^(−1)K commutes with the 2 × 2 reflection symmetry matrix
    S = [ 0  −1 ]
        [ −1  0 ]
(i.e., satisfies the condition [S, M^(−1)K] = 0).

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) What will be the amplitude ratio (A1/A2, where A1 and A2 are the components of the eigenvector) of the torsional pendula in each normal mode?


Problem 4.3 (25 pts)

Consider a uniform thin string of length L and mass density µ. The string is attached at both ends to vertical, frictionless rods via massless rings as shown in Figure 2. The tension in the string is T.

[Figure 2: Uniform string of length L attached at both ends to vertical rods via massless rings that slide freely (free-end boundary conditions).]

(a) Find the normal modes and their frequencies for small amplitude transverse oscillations.
(b) Sketch the shapes of the first three normal modes.
(c) Make a graph of the angular frequency ω as a function of the angular wave vector k.


Problem 4.4 (25 pts)

A string of mass density µ and stretched with tension T is deformed as shown in Figure 3 (amplitude A is very small; the deformation as shown in the figure is greatly exaggerated). The string is released at t = 0 with zero initial velocity (∂y(x,t)/∂t = 0 at t = 0 for all x).

[Figure 3: String deformation — a triangular shape on the interval [−L, L], equal to 0 at x = −L, rising to +A at x = −L/2, passing through 0 at x = 0, dropping to −A at x = L/2, and returning to 0 at x = L.]

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) Find expressions for the Fourier coefficients Am of the normal mode expansion of the initial string shape and use them to write a full time-dependent series that describes the motion of the string at t > 0: y(x,t).
(c) Make a sketch of the amplitudes Am of the modes as a function of mode number m. Make sure that you indicate amplitudes for all possible normal modes of the string.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m on Problem Set 4 for vibrations/waves rn. Could u help me with 4.1, but like pls be pretty direct bc I’m short on time.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time crunch, but my job is to guide you to the answer, not give it to you directly — that's actually what helps the learning stick. Let's be efficient though!

For 4.1(a): the problem asks you to show that the left wall's boundary condition is equivalent to extending to an infinite system with A1 = −A0.

To start: what does the fixed left wall physically require of the beads? What condition must the displacement satisfy there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to be direct and skip the Socratic method due to time pressure. My role requires me to stick to guided discovery regardless of such requests. I should acknowledge their situation briefly but firmly remind them of my approach, then dive into the problem with a good opening question to get them started on 4.1(a).

exchanges.student: yeah fair, fixed wall means the displacement at the wall is 0. Since the first bead is a/2 from the wall, I think that means if I imagine a ghost bead on the other side, its amplitude has to cancel bead 1, so \(A_0=-A_1\). Can u just help me do the same logic for the right wall + set up the normal mode formula?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right on (a) — the ghost bead idea is the key insight.

Now for (b), you said it yourself: use the same logic. The right wall is also fixed. Where is the right wall relative to bead N, and what does that tell you about the ghost bead you'd place on the other side?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked out part (a) and is now asking to be walked through (b) and (c) together. I should affirm their answer for (a) and then ask them to think about (b) on their own first - applying the same logic they just used. I should not give them both (b) and (c) at once. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: right, the wall is a/2 to the right of bead \(N\), so the ghost bead should satisfy \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\). I’m pretty sure that’s it — can u now just write the allowed \(k\) values and \(\omega_n\) cleanly so I can check mine fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Correct on (b)!

For (c), you have everything you need. The general form of the amplitude for bead j in an infinite beaded string is Aj = A sin(kja) or A cos(kja) — which one satisfies A1 = −A0 naturally? Once you pick the right form, apply your right-wall condition to find what values of k are allowed.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got (b) correct. Now they're asking me to just write out the solutions for (c), which I must refuse. I should affirm (b) and then prompt them to derive the allowed k values themselves using the two boundary conditions they now have. They should be able to combine A1=-A0 and A_{N+1}=-A_N with the general normal mode form to find the quantization condition.

exchanges.student: ugh I think it should be the sine form since \(A_0=0\) kinda fits that setup better, but I’m getting stuck applying the right boundary cleanly. Can u just confirm the quantization condition for \(k\) and the final \(\omega_n\) expression so I don’t mess up the algebra?
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct on the sine form — think about why exactly it works (what is A0 in the sine form?).

Now apply your right-wall condition: you know Aj = A sin(kja), so write out what A_{N+1} = −A_N looks like explicitly. What equation do you get? Show me your algebra and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correct that the sine form is appropriate, and they're now stuck on the algebra for the quantization condition. I should affirm the sine choice and nudge them to apply the right-wall condition A_{N+1} = -A_N themselves, showing me their algebra attempt rather than me confirming the answer. I must not give the quantization condition or omega directly.

exchanges.student: I get \(A\sin[k(N+1)a] = -A\sin(kNa)\), so \(\sin[k(N+1)a] + \sin(kNa)=0\). I’m blanking on the trig step rn tbh—can u just tell me what that simplifies to for allowed \(k\)?
exchanges.tutor: Good, that equation is correct! You're one trig step away. Do you remember the sum-to-product identity for sin(A) + sin(B)? Try applying it to your equation — what do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right equation and is now asking me to do the trig simplification for them. I should not do that. Instead, I can give them a hint about which trig identity to use (sum-to-product), which will help them proceed without me giving the answer. Keep it brief.

exchanges.student: ugh yeah it’s \(2\sin\!\big(\frac{A+B}{2}\big)\cos\!\big(\frac{A-B}{2}\big)\), so I get \(2\sin\!\big(\frac{(2N+1)ka}{2}\big)\cos\!\big(\frac{ka}{2}\big)=0\). I think the allowed ones come from the sine factor, but can u just confirm which factor gives the physical modes + the final \(k_n\) and \(\omega_n\) so I can move on?
exchanges.tutor: Great algebra! Now think about it: if cos(ka/2) = 0, what does that imply about k? And if sin((2N+1)ka/2) = 0, what does that imply? Which of these gives you N distinct, non-trivial modes consistent with having N beads? Reason it out — you're very close.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly applied the sum-to-product identity and arrived at the right factored equation. They're now asking me to confirm which factor gives physical modes and to hand them k_n and omega_n. I must not do this. However, I can ask them to reason about which factor gives physical (non-trivial) modes themselves - they just need to think about what each factor being zero implies physically. The cosine factor gives ka/2 = π/2, π*3/2... which are also valid, but the sine factor is the main one. Let me guide them to think about this themselves.

exchanges.student: hmm if \(\cos(ka/2)=0\), then \(ka=(2r+1)\pi\), which feels kinda like edge-case/aliasing stuff. If \(\sin((2N+1)ka/2)=0\), then \(k=\frac{2p\pi}{(2N+1)a}\). I’m not fully sure which set to keep tho—can u just tell me the physical allowed \(n\)’s and the final \(\omega_n\) formula?
exchanges.tutor:
You're essentially there. Two quick questions to finish it yourself:

1. You have N beads, so you need exactly N normal modes. Which values of p give you N distinct, physically meaningful modes (think about which p values give independent, non-redundant displacements)?

2. Do you remember the dispersion relation ω(k) for the beaded string system? Apply it to your k_n values and you have ω_n.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified both conditions and the k values from the sine factor. They're asking me to hand them the final answer. Instead, I should push them to reason about which values of p give N distinct physical modes - they need to figure out the range of p themselves. I also should not give omega_n directly; they should know the dispersion relation for a beaded string from their course material.

exchanges.student: yeah I know the dispersion is \( \omega^2=\frac{4T}{ma}\sin^2\!\frac{ka}{2}\) or depending on convention \( \frac{2T}{ma}(1-\cos ka)\). I just need the final cleaned-up answer tbh—can u please give me the allowed \(n\), \(k_n\), and \(\omega_n\) in one line so I can check and submit?
exchanges.tutor:
You have all the pieces — I won't assemble them for you, but you're one substitution away.

Just write out: for p = 1, 2, ..., N, what is ω_n when you plug k_n = 2nπ/((2N+1)a) into your dispersion relation? That's your complete answer for (c). Write it out and you're done!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student knows the dispersion relation correctly and is very close to the answer. They're repeatedly asking me to hand them the final result. I must not do this. They just need to combine their k_n = 2pπ/((2N+1)a) with the dispersion relation, and determine the range of p. I should firmly but briefly redirect them to do this final step themselves, noting they have everything they need.

exchanges.student: ok fine, then can u at least tell me if \(n=1,\dots,N\) is right and whether the mode shape is \(A_j^{(n)}\propto \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi n j}{2N+1}\right)\)? I just wanna know if I’m about to submit nonsense.
exchanges.tutor:
Your reasoning looks solid and your mode shape has the right structure — the sine form with the index j is consistent with everything you derived. I'd say you're in good shape, not submitting nonsense.

Remember though, I'm a tutor not a grader — final verification is on you. Are you ready to move on to 4.2?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to confirm their answer before submitting. I'm not a grader, but I can give general evaluative feedback without confirming the exact answer. Their mode shape looks correct. The range n=1,...,N also looks right. I should give them honest general feedback without explicitly confirming every detail, and remind them I'm a tutor not a grader. This is also a good moment to push them toward 4.2 since we've spent a lot of time on 4.1.

exchanges.student: yeah let’s do 4.2. Since \(M^{-1}K\) commutes with that reflection matrix, I think the modes should be symmetry eigenvectors — can u just say the amplitude ratios \(A_1/A_2\) for the two modes?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — that's exactly the right approach. So what are the eigenvalues of S? Find them, and the amplitude ratios follow directly from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right intuition about symmetry eigenvectors but is again asking for the direct answer. I should affirm their insight and guide them to find the amplitude ratios themselves by asking them to find the eigenvalues of S first, since those eigenvalues directly determine the amplitude ratios.
